pattern: Ignoring the palindrome rule, the value that minimizes total distance to every element is the median, so the true answer must be a palindrome sitting very close to that median -- only a handful of nearby palindromes need to be checked.
hints: Without any restriction on the target value, the cost-minimizing target for 'move everything to one number' is always the median of the array -- so the answer has to be a palindrome near that median.

Look at the median's digit count. Build candidate palindromes by taking the first half of its digits, then trying that half unchanged, one less, and one more, mirroring each into a full palindrome of the same length.

Don't forget the two boundary candidates where the digit count itself changes: the largest palindrome with one fewer digit (all nines), and the smallest palindrome with one more digit (a 1, some zeros, then a 1). Compute the total distance from every element to each candidate using sorted prefix sums and take the minimum.

edge_inputs.description: single element that is already a palindrome costs nothing
single element far from any small palindrome
largest allowed value, one below the smallest ten-digit number
several copies of the largest allowed value
median sits just above a power of ten, forcing the digit-length-change candidates to matter
median sits just below a power of ten from the other side
already uniform and palindromic, so zero cost
widely spread values forcing a real trade-off around the median

stress.description: large random array across the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large array clustered tightly around a random power-of-ten boundary to stress candidate generation
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    center = 10 ** rng.randint(1, 9)
    nums = [max(1, min(1000000000, center + rng.randint(-50, 50))) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minimumCost(self, nums):
        import bisect
        srt = sorted(nums)
        n = len(srt)
        prefix = [0] * (n + 1)
        for i, v in enumerate(srt):
            prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + v

        def cost(y):
            idx = bisect.bisect_left(srt, y)
            left_sum = prefix[idx]
            left_count = idx
            right_sum = prefix[n] - prefix[idx]
            right_count = n - idx
            return (y * left_count - left_sum) + (right_sum - y * right_count)

        # brute: try every palindrome under 10**9 (fewer than 120000 of them)
        best = None
        for length in range(1, 10):
            half_len = (length + 1) // 2
            start = 10 ** (half_len - 1) if half_len > 1 else 1
            end = 10 ** half_len
            for h in range(start, end):
                hs = str(h)
                s = hs + hs[::-1] if length % 2 == 0 else hs + hs[:-1][::-1]
                y = int(s)
                if y >= 10 ** 9:
                    continue
                c = cost(y)
                if best is None or c < best:
                    best = c
        return best
